figures.caption: Table 2: Performance comparison on MCUB benchmark [3]. SSAM achieves the best average performance across all modality combinations and consistently outperforms both training-free merging baselines and models finetuned on paired multimodal data.

Table 1: Performance comparison on MUSIC-AVQA [17] and AVQA [45] datasets. SSAM achieves the best average performance across all modality combinations and consistently outperforms both training-free model merging baselines and models finetuned on paired multimodal data.

Fig. 2: Overview of the proposed SSAM method. (a) Subspace projection: each language vector ∆i is projected onto a shared low-rank subspace prior to merging. (b) SSAM constructs covariance matrices A and B from all ∆i, extracts the shared left and right bases Uc and Vc, projects language vectors, and merge them into ∆merged.
Fig. 3: Qualitative examples on commonality understanding on the MCUB-4 dataset.
